sentences.text: Till today, I ask the same question.
It was not wise.
But there was-- there were not communication in the time.
They only thing is-- we went is rumors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- the rumors were, when the Gestapo comes in, they line up the men, and they kill them.
And I-- this was my explanation.
That's why my father, my older brothers, decided to leave.
But again, it was not wise, because they ran into difficulties.

When my father decided to go back home, they already ran into-- to the Germans and to the Gestapo; to a point when they stopped, they say, children under 16 go on this side, and all the men go in the truck.
This was already-- they were running to the direction towards the backed, you know, town.
They didn't know where they were running, 'cause they didn't know from what direction the German will come in.
And there were tens of thousand of refugees on the roads, running from the German.
But the Germans came in from different direction, which a lot of people got stuck in the middle.
There were cars, and there were buses, and there were trucks driving and getting stuck, 'cause they ran out of gas.
But my family had a horse and wagons.
And during their absence, they-- they were taken by the Germans, and to a point that they took my father and they ask him to dig his grave to be shot.
But my older brother, which is next to me, got so hysterical, crying and screaming.
In some way he succeeded to run away with my father and join the rest of the family-- the other two brothers.
And there they continue till they came to our town.
Yes.
They just left.
And I kinda felt, I'll be the man of the house-- responsible-- you knows?
But they were just away for a few days.
We were lucky, you know, that our house, because it was the biggest house, became the headquarters of the German army.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed on the bottom floor, first floor.
And the-- the German stay in the whole house.
And I remember they were [BACKROUND NOISE] giving us chocolates.
And they were very friendly at night and the daytime, to an extent that they advised us to leave as soon as possible-- possible tomorrow, possible a day later-- the Gestapo comes in.
When the Gestapo comes in, they have to leave town.
The army has to leave town.
And the whole family decided to leave.
Yeah.
The Germany-- German were there.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I cannot explain why.
But the only thing we were thinking-- they were Austrian soldiers from Austria.
And we felt they're nice people.
Why?
Don't know till today.
They were just nice.
They advised us.
We were very friendly.
I remember my mother used to cook for them, used to bring fruit from the gardens.
And it's like, you know, we were a part of them.
We were one of the first one to leave.
And then I would say 90% of the community left.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- there were about-- they claim, about 30 left in-- in-- in town.
And what happened to them when the Gestapo came in, they took horses and rope, and they tied up the Jews.
And they were running around in town with them, you know, schlepping them along.
And whoever they didn't find, they lined them up on a-- in a area on the way to the cemetery, which they shot 'em and burned 'em.
And I didn't know until 1989, because when I came in 1989, I wanted to go to the cemetery.
When I asked the Polish people on the way to the cemetery, they told me that the cemetery was not in existence.
They wiped out the cemetery.
The only thing you'll find is a tomb in memory of the last Jews which were shot and burned-- very small tomb.
Definitely.
I would say they took a big part of it in our town, because I would say it was a small town.
There were little towns around it.
And they definitely took part of it-- no question about it.
They just-- I don't know what hit 'em.
And maybe the young people were lookin' for excitement.
There was-- I cannot see otherwise-- to look for revenge?
Everybody worked.
Everybody made a living, especially, you know, my father.
But just, they did it like they did it all over Europe.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by being there in '89-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the town looked worse before we left.
The Russian never did anything to it.
Matter of fact, they took down many buildings.
I remember it used to be the court building, the jail building, which they made a-- they made a bus stop, big area, you know, for cars.
And we walked around in town.
And, you know-- and what I also wanted to do-- I remembered that when we left, you know, the town, my family buried a lot of valuable things.
And I remember, you know-- very exciting what they're doing.
And I watched it.
And in my mind, I wanted to go.
And I-- I knew exactly.
It made a very big impression on me-- where they buried it, what corner.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we became very friendly to the driver, that we had him all the time.
And I had-- I was telling him.
And I drove over, not once, many, many times.
And I pointed out.
I say, here.
This is the spot that I wanna go and dig.
I had a shovel.
And I remember beautiful things, which-- I remember, my mother used to have a tremendous candelabra.
And they buried gold, and they buried different-- any valuable things they didn't want to take along, they buried it there in big craters, you know, covered and everything.
But when I came, it was already towards the evening.
You know, the digs were small.
And I said to my driver, I'm gonna do it.
He says, if you gonna do it, I'll leave you here.
Because I don't want to be present, because they're gonna catch you.
Because it was still under communist regime.
They'll put you in jail.
It'll take six months till anybody will come and get you or find out that you're in jail.
Don't do it.
And I-- I left.
I left the scene.
Then I felt kind of-- I'm gonna have a coffee and anything.
And I remind myself-- I remember, there was a big bar not far from the shul.
And we went there.
It was on a Sunday.
And bars are open on Sundays.
I went into the bar.
And the driver says, you know, they would like to have something to eat and drink.
And they say, we have bread.
We have coffee.
But we don't have butter, 'cause they're not allowed to sell butter on the bar-- just for cooking.
But he said, but I'll try to give you butter.
We had you know, bread, and butter, and coffee.
Then suddenly, I-- and there were Polish there drinking.
All of them were drunk.
Suddenly, I noticed that the driver's not with us.
And I got up, and I walked out.
I saw he was standing with two policemen.
And I right away walked over, and I said, and boys is-- is anything wrong?
Can I help you?
Because he is our driver.
And I said to them, if it has anything to do with us, ask me.
Don't ask my driver.
And I said, I'm a-- I'm born this town, and I came from the United States.
And I took out the passport, and I show it to him.
And-- and I'm, you know, visiting the town.
And they were kind of very apologetic.
And they said, we had a call from this bar that strange people are here.
And they have a movie camera and taking pictures.
And he apologized, and he left.
But when we left, we saw it kind of get dark again.
And I still wanted to go back to that place and try to dig.
Again, he gave me a hard time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Just, my father was the leader.
He made the decision.
And he decided, he says, from now on, we're all gonna be together.
We're not gonna be apart anymore, because I made a mistake, and I don't want to do it again.
And we load up very fast.
We took whatever we could.
And we were heading to the direction where the Russian occupied Poland.
But again, rumors, which were the war was still going on, the Polish army was fighting very bitterly.
And during our travels, we've seen thousands of Polish soldiers on the roads, on the fields.
There was a war going on, you know-- shooting, grenades, everything.
But we manage to cross the lake.
They used to call the lake [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
We had a hard time, but we managed to cross the lake and continued to the direction to the Russian side.
Because we felt that-- actually, we did not make a decision on our own.
Again, from rumors that people are running away towards Russia, the Russian side.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we stopped by, still on the German side, in a smaller town for a couple days.
And we decided to continue to a bigger town, which the name was Strie.
And Strie was a big, big city.
And there were a lot of military camps, Polish military camps, which my brother, Isador, was stationed there.
He went up to me and reasoned.
He said, it's a big city, and it's in the Russian side, and I think, too far.
It's close.
And let's move in the direction to Strie.
Horse and wagons.
We-- some of us walked.
There were areas which mud-- we had to push the wagons.
But most of the time, we were all on the wagons.
Yeah.
There were-- it was my nephew, my oldest sister's son, my two brother-in-laws.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
We were all together.
We kept together or as-- as one group, from the beginning of the war-- many years after.
And we end up in Strie; which we rented an apartment.
Again, we all lived together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we kinda settled.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We went to school.
But it didn't last too long.
Within less than a year we were awaken by the Russian in [NON-ENGLISH].
And told us to take as little as possible-- belonging.
And they put us on trucks.
And they brought us to a train station.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which were-- and we were loaded to railroad cars, freight railroad cars-- put in this like cattle.
And we didn't know where we were going.
And it took less than a week.
I would save five to six days we were traveling in the railroad cars, without food.
What they used to give us-- a glass of water and a piece of bread every day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the train used to stop over at different stations.
We were watched by the Russian army.
They were, you know, traveling along with us.
But they let us jump off the cars and buy certain things.
And we managed to arrive in Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Why?
What we were thinking, being in Strie, the Russian had approached us to become Russian citizen-- not only us.
But all of the people who came in to settle in Strie had refused to become Russian citizens, because they were afraid.
In case the war ends, and the Polish will find out that we switched our citizenship towards Russia, you know, we'll-- there'll be a lot of trouble.
And we felt that's why.
They-- they were ignored and just took every Jew in the area-- midnight when they took us away to Siberia.
I was like 13 years old, you know.
During my travels.
There were no bar mitzvah, you know.
I remember we're-- they were celebrating my bar mitzvah, and-- just the family and a few people.
And they send me out to buy two bottles of beer.
There used to be the big quarts of beer.
I was so happy they send me to buy the beer.
And I-- when I came back, I tried to put it on-- on the table.
One of the bottles exploded.
I'll never forget that.
This was my bar mitzvah.
It was in some-- I think it was in Strie, if I'm remembering.
But it's-- it wasn't, you know, 1/2 an hour, if that.
But I went to shul, put on the [NON-ENGLISH].
This was-- this was the big event.
Yeah
Yeah, yeah.
They were taking us to a point to be transferred to an area, which was called Tiger.
The natives of that area where we were talking about were telling us, whoever ever went in, never came back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were no roads.
We had to go on logs, big logs-- they put us on.
And that's how we were supposed to go.
And this was in Ural-- the area.
And I remember it was very exciting.
There was no night-- always day.
There was no night.
And there were tens of thousands of people sitting in the area and waiting to be transferred to the Tiger area.
But because we heard those rumors, we try-- my parents tried that to do anything and everything not to go there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened was that my older brother got very sick.
My brother, Israel-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And he got very high temperature.
And when they took him to the clinic, the doctor said, he must go to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and they said, some of the families have to go along with him.
But we all succeeded to go along with him.
15 people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And naturally, this was our luck that he got sick, because the conditions were very bad.
There was no food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We didn't have no beds, no blankets-- just freezing.
The only thing we kept warm, we were making, you know, fires, you know, warm up.
We were there for about two days.
That's horrible.
But from there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --we manage-- to get-- to a-- they transferred us to an area near Sverdlovsk-- big city.
And they took us to the camps, which the camp was surrounded by barbed wire-- and told us, this is the area where you're gonna live.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There-- there were no, you know, living quarters.
There were no barracks.
There was some little homes which were not available, you know, to be rented or, you know, live there.
Then my father-- my family decided to build his own barrack.
And we managed to build a barrack with three rooms and a little kitchen on the side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The area was right near a big forest.
This was, I remember, coldest months.
And they told us, here.
Here are the trees.
You're welcome to go in, cut the trees, and build a house.
And because of the background, you know, the lumber business, we built a house, and we lived there.
We lived there over a year.
Because they kept as-- as traitors, 'cause we refused to become Russian citizens.
And they treated us as prisoners.
We were not allowed to get out of camp.
Whatever we did is work.
Get pay-- I'm not-- we, I mean my older brothers-- get pay, and the pay went to-- to the Russian, which were in charge, to pay them off to treat us better, to give us a chance to buy food, whatever.
